title: Lateral Movement with Stolen Session (Linux)
briefing: $TARGET accepted an SSH public-key login for $USER1 from $SRC1 — but $SRC1 is another INTERNAL host, not the internet. Moments later the session escalated to root. An adversary who already owns one box is pivoting east-west with a reused key/session. Confirm the lateral movement and scope how far it may have spread.
dashboard_hint: rule.id 5715 with data.srcip in RFC1918 space ($SRC1 is internal) landing on $TARGET, then 5402/5403 (sudo to root, group sudo). Internal-to-internal successful auth is normal for admins but suspicious off-hours from a host that has no business SSHing here. Correlate: is $SRC1 itself already flagged? Where else has $USER1 logged in from $SRC1?

options: Lateral movement — an internal host pivoting to $TARGET with valid creds
A user resetting their own password
questions.answer: Lateral movement — an internal host pivoting to $TARGET with valid creds
questions.explain: A successful SSH key login sourced from an internal RFC1918 host, then root, is east-west movement using stolen/reused credentials.
All of the activity lands on $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: What is the key tell that this is lateral movement, not internet-facing attack?
options: The source IP is an internal RFC1918 address, not external
questions.answer: The source IP is an internal RFC1918 address, not external
questions.explain: data.srcip is a private/internal address — the attacker is already inside and hopping between hosts.

questions.prompt: Internal host the login came from (the pivot)?
questions.explain: data.srcip = $SRC1, the internal pivot host — itself likely compromised.
data.dstuser = $USER1 on the accepted publickey login.
SSH lateral movement, valid-account use, and sudo privesc. No public-facing exploit is involved — the actor is already internal.

options: Investigate and isolate the pivot host $SRC1 too
Revoke $USER1's SSH keys everywhere and rotate the account
Hunt every host $USER1/$SRC1 has authenticated to
answer: Investigate and isolate the pivot host $SRC1 too
Revoke $USER1's SSH keys everywhere and rotate the account
Hunt every host $USER1/$SRC1 has authenticated to
questions.explain: Both ends are suspect. Scope the blast radius by following the account and the pivot host across the fleet; external firewall rules do nothing against east-west movement.
